papers.title: TempCompass: Do Video LLMs Really Understand Videos?
papers.figureCaption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.
papers.subtitle: ACL 2024 | diagnostic temporal benchmark
papers.thesis: TempCompass is the cleanest starting point for asking whether a Video LLM really understands time, not just static content.
papers.bigPicture: The right reading strategy is to watch how the paper turns diagnostic temporal benchmark into a concrete research problem, then see whether the setup and experiments actually support that framing.
papers.problemFrame: It addresses the lack of benchmarks that separate different temporal aspects and task formats, so models can no longer hide behind static-frame bias or a single QA style.
papers.authorClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The core message is diagnostic rather than architectural: current benchmarks do not adequately measure temporal perception, so TempCompass creates a broader testbed and shows that existing models are weak on it.
papers.researchQuestion: It addresses the lack of benchmarks that separate different temporal aspects and task formats, so models can no longer hide behind static-frame bias or a single QA style.
papers.coreClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The core message is diagnostic rather than architectural: current benchmarks do not adequately measure temporal perception, so TempCompass creates a broader testbed and shows that existing models are weak on it.
papers.methodSetup: TempCompass combines conflicting video pairs, multiple temporal aspect categories, and an instruction generation pipeline designed to produce richer evaluation prompts.
papers.coreInsight: Read this paper as a benchmark-design lesson: before trusting any score gain, ask whether the benchmark really forces temporal evidence instead of static shortcuts or language priors.
papers.readerValue: Use this paper to calibrate what should count as a real temporal improvement before you read stronger method papers.
papers.whyItMatters: TempCompass tells us whether a model is relying on static scene cues instead of temporal evidence, which is exactly the kind of hidden shortcut that often looks like temporal hallucination.

papers.title: Video-MME: The First-Ever Comprehensive Evaluation Benchmark of Multi-modal LLMs in Video Analysis
papers.figureCaption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.
papers.subtitle: arXiv 2024 | comprehensive multimodal video benchmark
papers.thesis: Video-MME is the broad field-standard benchmark you read when you want to know how strong a model looks on real video analysis across domains, durations, and modalities, not only on narrowly temporal tasks.
papers.bigPicture: The right reading strategy is to watch how the paper turns comprehensive multimodal video benchmark into a concrete evaluation problem, then ask what this broad setup measures well and what it still leaves ambiguous about temporal evidence use.
papers.problemFrame: The field lacked a comprehensive and carefully annotated benchmark for evaluating multimodal LLMs on video analysis across short and long videos, different domains, and multiple input modalities.
papers.authorClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The central message is that the field needed a comprehensive benchmark for multimodal video analysis, and Video-MME provides broad coverage over domains, durations, modalities, and quality-controlled annotations.
papers.researchQuestion: It tries to fix the lack of a comprehensive and carefully annotated benchmark for evaluating multimodal LLMs on video analysis across short and long videos, different domains, and multiple input modalities.
papers.coreClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The central message is that the field needed a comprehensive benchmark for multimodal video analysis, and Video-MME provides broad coverage over domains, durations, modalities, and quality-controlled annotations.
papers.methodSetup: Video-MME builds a benchmark around 900 manually selected videos, 2,700 question-answer pairs, multiple duration bands, multiple visual domains, and multiple modality configurations including subtitles and audio.
papers.coreInsight: Read this as a benchmark-scope lesson: broad coverage is useful for field-wide calibration, but it does not by itself guarantee that the model truly used temporal evidence rather than subtitles, audio, or broad scene priors.
papers.readerValue: Read this in Week 1 because it teaches you what much of the field currently means by video understanding; after that, TempCompass becomes easier to appreciate as a stricter temporal stress test instead of just another benchmark.
papers.whyItMatters: Video-MME matters because it is the broad benchmark many later papers report, so it tells you how much a model looks good under realistic multimodal evaluation, but it also reminds you that broad success does not automatically mean temporally faithful reasoning.

By the end of the introduction, you should be able to say why this paper became a field-standard reporting benchmark.

points: One-sentence method or benchmark description: Video-MME builds a benchmark around 900 manually selected videos, 2,700 question-answer pairs, multiple duration bands, multiple visual domains, and multiple modality configurations including subtitles and audio.

steps.summary: Do not stare at the leaderboard first. Look at what the breakdowns imply about the benchmark's actual pressure points.
points: Experimental headline: The experiments compare commercial and open-source multimodal models under different duration and modality settings and show that broad video analysis remains difficult, especially as video length and multimodal complexity grow.
The key question is not only who wins, but whether the performance breakdown tells you what modality or context the model is leaning on.
After reading the results, ask whether the benchmark is measuring temporal reasoning, broad multimodal competence, or both at once.

readingHighlights: The benchmark spans diverse visual domains and subfields, so it aims to represent broad video analysis rather than one narrow task family.
It explicitly varies video duration from short clips to hour-long videos, which matters for contextual and temporal stress.
It evaluates multiple modality settings, including video-only, subtitles, and audio, to reveal what models really use.
The dataset is manually selected and annotated at a higher quality bar than many broad benchmark collections.
The most useful reading move is to compare what Video-MME measures well against what TempCompass measures more cleanly.

summaryNotes.introduction: The introduction is really about benchmark breadth: existing evaluation was too fragmented to tell whether multimodal LLMs were genuinely ready for broad video understanding.
summaryNotes.method: Video-MME builds a benchmark around 900 manually selected videos, 2,700 question-answer pairs, multiple duration bands, multiple visual domains, and multiple modality configurations including subtitles and audio.
summaryNotes.experiments: The experiments compare commercial and open-source multimodal models under the benchmark's different settings and show clear performance gaps, especially when video gets longer or when broader multimodal understanding is required.
summaryNotes.limitations: It is intentionally broad, so it is less surgical than TempCompass about isolating whether the model truly used temporal evidence rather than subtitles, audio, or broad scene priors.

papers.title: TimeChat: A Time-sensitive Multimodal Large Language Model for Long Video Understanding
papers.figureCaption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.
papers.subtitle: CVPR 2024 | timestamp-aware video LLM
papers.thesis: TimeChat is a method paper that tries to make a Video LLM care more explicitly about time, not just about scene semantics.
papers.bigPicture: The right reading strategy is to watch how the paper turns timestamp-aware video LLM into a concrete research problem, then see whether the setup and experiments actually support that framing.
papers.problemFrame: It targets the mismatch between long-video understanding and weak timestamp sensitivity, where the model may summarize content without grounding it in time.
papers.authorClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The abstract frames timestamp awareness as necessary for long-video understanding and positions the model as time-sensitive rather than just language-capable.
papers.researchQuestion: It targets the mismatch between long-video understanding and weak timestamp sensitivity, where the model may summarize content without grounding it in time.
papers.coreClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The abstract frames timestamp awareness as necessary for long-video understanding and positions the model as time-sensitive rather than just language-capable.
papers.methodSetup: The method builds a time-sensitive multimodal model intended to improve how temporal information is tracked over long videos.
papers.coreInsight: The core insight is that understanding is incomplete until the answer is anchored to the right time span; fluent language is not enough.
papers.readerValue: Use this paper to sharpen your taste for temporal grounding: not just whether the answer sounds right, but whether it is tied to the correct span.
papers.whyItMatters: If a model cannot bind answers to the right time, it may produce fluent but unsupported temporal claims, which is a common form of temporal hallucination.

papers.title: MASH-VLM: Mitigating Action-Scene Hallucination in Video-LLMs through Disentangled Spatial-Temporal Representations
papers.figureCaption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.
papers.subtitle: CVPR 2025 | hallucination mitigation
papers.thesis: MASH-VLM argues that action-scene hallucination comes from entangled spatial-temporal features and positional bias, then attacks both directly.
papers.bigPicture: The right reading strategy is to watch how the paper turns hallucination mitigation into a concrete research problem, then see whether the setup and experiments actually support that framing.
papers.problemFrame: It targets cases where a Video LLM predicts an action from scene context or infers a scene from an observed action incorrectly.
papers.authorClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The abstract makes a compact causal claim: if spatial and temporal signals are entangled too early, the model is more likely to hallucinate action-scene associations.
papers.researchQuestion: It targets cases where a Video LLM predicts an action from scene context or infers a scene from an observed action incorrectly.
papers.coreClaim: The paper's main claim is this: The abstract makes a compact causal claim: if spatial and temporal signals are entangled too early, the model is more likely to hallucinate action-scene associations.
papers.methodSetup: The model combines DST-attention with Harmonic-RoPE so that spatial and temporal information stay more separated while still aligning with text.
papers.coreInsight: The paper sharpens an important point: many apparent reasoning failures are actually failures to represent order, transition, or duration in a stable way.
papers.readerValue: This paper helps you separate order confusion from broader reasoning failure, which is valuable when analyzing temporal hallucination.
papers.whyItMatters: It is useful because many temporal hallucinations are actually entanglement errors, where scene context leaks into event prediction or event order gets overridden by coarse spatial cues.

Harmonic-RoPE changes positional handling so text, spatial, and temporal tokens stay better balanced.
The UNSCENE benchmark has 1,320 videos and 4,078 QA pairs.
The method achieves strong results on UNSCENE and also improves general video understanding benchmarks.
